text: The mass at the apex of the chest is first approached by incising the pleura circumferentially.
Then the mass is dissected away from attaching tissues using bipolar electrocautery.
To facilitate dissecting the mass, a single stitch is placed thoracoscopically to allow retraction of the mass anteriorly to avoid dissection of the sympathetic ganglia.
The posterior attachments of the mass are then easily divided using the bipolar cautery.
The mass is then placed in an endocatch bag and removed from the chest.
Intercostal rib blocks are performed from approximately T2 to T8.
An MRI helped better characterize the mass, abutting the T1 nerve root, but not involving the vertebral foramina.
Once again, the case is begun by circumferentially dividing the pleura using the hook electrocautery.
Also important to note is that selectively during this case, smoke is allowed to escape through one of the laparoscopic ports to allow better visualization.
Most interestingly, once the mass was retracted laterally, you can appreciate the T1 nerve root, which abuts the mass.
This root is carefully dissected out from the surrounding tissue.
This angle highlights the ability to move the camera from the inferior port to the posterior port to provide better visualization posterior to the mass.
This angle provides a good exposure of the collapsed left lung as a result of the CO2 insufflation.
Care is made to not over insufflate the chest to cause hypotension.
Once again, the posterior attachments of the tumor were hard to reach.
Thus, a suture was placed thoracoscopically through the tumor, this time using a number two microsuture.
Clips are placed on the suture, and this is used for retraction.
This case reinforces the importance of the use of a 30-degree scope to allow visualization around bulky lesions.
The mass is then eventually completely removed from this apical space.
The mass is then placed in an endocatch bag and removed from the chest.
